Major League Soccer began to demonstrate positive signs of long-term profitability as early as 2004 with the single-entity ownership structure, salary cap, and the media and marketing umbrella Soccer United Marketing (SUM) all contributing towards MLS' financial security.[[31]](#cite_note-nytimes-33) As soccer-specific stadiums were built, ownership expanded, and television coverage increased, MLS saw its revenues increase while controlling costs.[[8]](#cite_note-forbes.com-10)

Television coverage and revenue have increased since the league's early years. In 2006, MLS reached an 8-year TV deal with [ESPN](/wiki/ESPN "ESPN") spanning the 2007–2014 seasons, and marked the first time that MLS earned rights fees, reported to be worth $7–8 million annually.[[230]](#cite_note-235) In September 2012 the league extended its distribution agreement with London-based Media rights agency MP & Silva until 2014 in a deal worth $10 million annually. Total league TV revenues are over $40 million annually.[[231]](#cite_note-236)[[232]](#cite_note-237) In 2011, MLS earned $150 million when it sold a 25% stake in SUM.[[8]](#cite_note-forbes.com-10)

In early 2005, MLS signed a 10-year, $150 million sponsorship deal with [Adidas](/wiki/Adidas "Adidas") for its jerseys and other equipment.[[31]](#cite_note-nytimes-33) In 2007, MLS teams started selling ad space on the front of jerseys to go along with the league-wide sponsorship partners who had already been advertising on the back of club jerseys, following the practice of international sport, specifically soccer. MLS established a floor of $500,000 per shirt sponsorship, with the league receiving a flat fee of $200,000 per deal.[[262]](#cite_note-267) As of July 2014, sixteen teams had signed sponsorship deals to have company logos placed on the front of their jerseys (and another team is directly owned by its shirt sponsor), and the league average from jersey sponsors was about $2.4 million.[[263]](#cite_note-268) Sleeve sponsorship was introduced to MLS in the 2020 season, with the teams able to sell a 2-by-2-inch (51 by 51 mm) section on the right arm where the league logo patch is normally positioned.[[264]](#cite_note-269)

The LA Galaxy made a profit in 2003 in their first season at The Home Depot Center,[[30]](#cite_note-profit-32) and FC Dallas turned a profit after moving into Pizza Hut Park in 2005.[[265]](#cite_note-thriving-270) For each season between 2006 and 2009, two to three MLS clubs (generally clubs with a soccer-specific stadium) were reported as profitable by the league.[[265]](#cite_note-thriving-270)[[266]](#cite_note-271)[[267]](#cite_note-272) In November 2013, *[Forbes](/wiki/Forbes "Forbes")* published a report that revealed that ten of the league's nineteen teams earned an operating profit in 2012, while two broke even and seven had a loss. Forbes estimated that the league's collective annual revenues were $494 million, and that the league's collective annual profit was $34 million. Forbes valued the league's franchises to be worth $103 million on average, almost three times as much as the $37 million average valuation in 2008. The Seattle Sounders FC franchise was named the most valuable at $175 million, a 483% gain over the $30 million league entrance fee it paid in 2009.[[8]](#cite_note-forbes.com-10)

The trend in increased team values has continued with MLS teams seeing a strong 52% increase in franchise values from 2012 to 2014. In August 2015 *Forbes* updated its MLS franchise values with the most profitable team measuring $245 million and the least $105 million. The average value jumped from $103 to $157 million.[[9]](#cite_note-forbes-2015-08-11) In 2018, *Forbes* estimated Atlanta United FC is the most valuable MLS team, worth $330 million, while the Colorado Rapids are the lowest value, at $155 million.[[268]](#cite_note-273) These valuations do not include the value of stadiums or training facilities owned by the respective clubs. A *[Sportico](/wiki/Sportico "Sportico")* ranking of club valuations in 2024 placed 20 MLS teams in the top 50 globally, with [Los Angeles FC](/wiki/Los_Angeles_FC "Los Angeles FC") the most valuable at $1.15 billion (15th overall).[[269]](#cite_note-274)

Prior to the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic"), MLS teams typically used commercial flights to transport players and staff between matches, with only four [charter flights](/wiki/Charter_flight "Charter flight") allowed under league rules.[[270]](#cite_note-275) These commercial flights were often non-direct, requiring transfers and layovers, and contributed to long travel days.[[271]](#cite_note-276) The number of charters allowed for league matches was increased to eight legs prior to the 2020 season and lifted entirely due to the COVID-19 pandemic.[[272]](#cite_note-277)[[273]](#cite_note-278) [Sun Country Airlines](/wiki/Sun_Country_Airlines "Sun Country Airlines") has provided charter service to MLS teams since 2020 and became the league's official carrier in 2022.[[274]](#cite_note-279)

### Rules and officials

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Major_League_Soccer&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Rules and officials")]

When the league began play, it tried to gain popularity by "Americanizing" the game: the game clock counted down in each half and stopped for certain dead ball, and matches level at the end of regulation were resolved with a running penalty shootout.[[275]](#cite_note-280) Now MLS follows the [rules and standards](/wiki/Laws_of_the_Game_(association_football) "Laws of the Game (association football)") of the [International Football Association Board](/wiki/International_Football_Association_Board "International Football Association Board") (IFAB).
Since 2005, the playoff extra time structure follows IFAB standards: two full 15-minute periods, followed by a [penalty shootout](/wiki/Penalty_shoot-out_(association_football) "Penalty shoot-out (association football)") if necessary.

[U.S. Soccer](/wiki/United_States_Soccer_Federation "United States Soccer Federation") hired the first full-time professional referees in league history in 2007 as part of the league's "Game First" initiatives.[[276]](#cite_note-281)
Major League Soccer has been implementing fines and suspensions since the 2011 season for simulation (diving) through its Disciplinary Committee, which reviews plays after the match. The first player fined under the new rule was [Charlie Davies](/wiki/Charlie_Davies "Charlie Davies"), fined $1,000 for intentionally deceiving match officials.[[277]](#cite_note-282)

MLS uses the list of banned substances published by the [World Anti-Doping Agency](/wiki/World_Anti-Doping_Agency "World Anti-Doping Agency").[[278]](#cite_note-283)

### Branding

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Major_League_Soccer&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Branding")]

The current MLS logo debuted in 2014, ahead of the league's 20th season, replacing an earlier logo that featured a stylized boot and ball. The current logo is a simple crest with a diagonal stripe, the MLS wordmark, and three stars that represent "community, club, and country". The logo was designed to be remixed in different color schemes that match teams when used on merchandise and jerseys.[[279]](#cite_note-284)

The first MLS anthem was unveiled in 2007 and was composed by [Audiobrain](/wiki/Audiobrain "Audiobrain"). The current league anthem debuted in 2020 and was composed by film score composer [Hans Zimmer](/wiki/Hans_Zimmer "Hans Zimmer"). It's used during league broadcasts and as a prelude to kickoff at stadiums.[[280]](#cite_note-285)

#### Team names

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Major_League_Soccer&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Team names")]

For more information on MLS team names, see the individual team entries.

In the early years of MLS, teams were typically given official nicknames in the style of other U.S. sports leagues (e.g., [Columbus Crew](/wiki/Columbus_Crew "Columbus Crew"), [Los Angeles Galaxy](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Galaxy "Los Angeles Galaxy"), [New England Revolution](/wiki/New_England_Revolution "New England Revolution")). Several club names in MLS originated with previous professional soccer clubs, such as the 1970s-era [NASL](/wiki/North_American_Soccer_League_(1968%E2%80%9384) "North American Soccer League (1968–84)") team names [San Jose Earthquakes](/wiki/San_Jose_Earthquakes_(1974%E2%80%9388) "San Jose Earthquakes (1974–88)"), [Seattle Sounders](/wiki/Seattle_Sounders_(1974%E2%80%9383) "Seattle Sounders (1974–83)"), [Portland Timbers](/wiki/Portland_Timbers_(1975%E2%80%9382) "Portland Timbers (1975–82)"), and [Vancouver Whitecaps](/wiki/Vancouver_Whitecaps_(1974%E2%80%9384) "Vancouver Whitecaps (1974–84)").[[281]](#cite_note-286)

[D.C. United](/wiki/D.C._United "D.C. United") was the only MLS team to adopt European naming conventions during the 1990s.[[282]](#cite_note-dc_united_name-287) In more recent years, European-style names have become increasingly common in MLS, with expansion teams such as [Real Salt Lake](/wiki/Real_Salt_Lake "Real Salt Lake"), [Toronto FC](/wiki/Toronto_FC "Toronto FC"), [New York City FC](/wiki/New_York_City_FC "New York City FC"), [Atlanta United FC](/wiki/Atlanta_United_FC "Atlanta United FC"), [Minnesota United FC](/wiki/Minnesota_United_FC "Minnesota United FC"), and [FC Cincinnati](/wiki/FC_Cincinnati "FC Cincinnati"), along with rebrandings such as [FC Dallas](/wiki/FC_Dallas "FC Dallas") (formerly the Dallas Burn),[[283]](#cite_note-288) [Sporting Kansas City](/wiki/Sporting_Kansas_City "Sporting Kansas City") (formerly the Kansas City Wizards),[[284]](#cite_note-289) and [CF Montréal](/wiki/CF_Montr%C3%A9al "CF Montréal") (formerly the Montreal Impact).

Austrian beverage company [Red Bull GmbH](/wiki/Red_Bull_GmbH "Red Bull GmbH") owns and sponsors the [New York Red Bulls](/wiki/New_York_Red_Bulls "New York Red Bulls") as well as other sports teams outside the U.S.[[159]](#cite_note-nytimes.com-164)

## Media coverage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Major_League_Soccer&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Media coverage")]

Main article: [Major League Soccer on television](/wiki/Major_League_Soccer_on_television "Major League Soccer on television")

See also: [List of current Major League Soccer broadcasters](/wiki/List_of_current_Major_League_Soccer_broadcasters "List of current Major League Soccer broadcasters")

### MLS Season Pass on Apple TV

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Major_League_Soccer&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: MLS Season Pass on Apple TV")]

Since 2023, all MLS and [Leagues Cup](/wiki/Leagues_Cup "Leagues Cup") matches, as well as certain matches from [MLS Next Pro](/wiki/MLS_Next_Pro "MLS Next Pro") and [MLS Next](/wiki/MLS_Next "MLS Next"), are streamed worldwide on [MLS Season Pass](/wiki/MLS_Season_Pass "MLS Season Pass") via [Apple TV](/wiki/Apple_TV_app "Apple TV app"). This agreement ended the previous regional sports network-based system.[[285]](#cite_note-290) The contract allows for some broadcasts on linear television. ESPN and Univision exited negotiations, apparently because MLS would not allow them to stream via their own platforms or use their own commentators.[[103]](#cite_note-Gostomelsky-105)[[286]](#cite_note-291) Following their departures, Fox Sports joined Apple as MLS' linear broadcast partners in the U.S., with [Bell Media](/wiki/Bell_Media "Bell Media")'s [TSN](/wiki/The_Sports_Network "The Sports Network") and [RDS](/wiki/R%C3%A9seau_des_sports "Réseau des sports") doing so in Canada.[[287]](#cite_note-292)

### United States

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Major_League_Soccer&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: United States")]

From 2012 to 2014, MLS matches were broadcast by [NBC Sports](/wiki/NBC_Sports "NBC Sports"), with 40 matches per year—primarily on [NBCSN](/wiki/NBCSN "NBCSN"), and select matches broadcast on the NBC network.[[288]](#cite_note-293) The move from [Fox Soccer](/wiki/Fox_Soccer "Fox Soccer") to the more widely distributed NBCSN caused viewership numbers to double for the 2012 season.[[289]](#cite_note-294)

Soccer United Marketing partnered with [Google](/wiki/Google "Google") and Bedrocket Media Ventures in 2012 to launch "KickTV", a premium [YouTube](/wiki/YouTube "YouTube") channel with original soccer programming.[[290]](#cite_note-295) KickTV was sold to [Copa90](/wiki/Copa90 "Copa90") in 2015 to form its American branch.[[291]](#cite_note-296) In 2020, Soccer United Marketing signed a multi-year agreement with [Bleacher Report](/wiki/Bleacher_Report "Bleacher Report") to produce content and highlights for MLS and the U.S. national teams through the 2022 season.[[292]](#cite_note-297)

From 2015 to 2022, MLS matches were broadcast nationally by [ESPN](/wiki/ESPN "ESPN") networks and [Fox Sports](/wiki/Fox_Sports_(United_States) "Fox Sports (United States)") in English, and [Univision](/wiki/Univision "Univision") networks in Spanish under an eight-year contract. Each broadcaster had a window for national regular season matches, with [UniMás](/wiki/UniM%C3%A1s "UniMás") airing a game on Friday nights in Spanish and additional matches on [Univision Deportes Network](/wiki/Univision_Deportes_Network "Univision Deportes Network"), and [ESPN](/wiki/ESPN_Major_League_Soccer "ESPN Major League Soccer") and [Fox Sports 1](/wiki/Fox_Sports_1 "Fox Sports 1") airing matches on Sunday evenings in English. ESPN, FS1, and Univision shared coverage of the playoffs, while ABC and Fox alternated broadcasting the MLS Cup final in English. In total, at least 125 matches were aired per season across all three networks. The three contracts have an average estimated value of $90 million per season—five times larger than the average $18 million value of the previous contracts with ESPN, Univision, and [NBC Sports](/wiki/NBC_Sports "NBC Sports").[[293]](#cite_note-philly-espnfs1mls-298)[[294]](#cite_note-nyt-newcontract-299)[[295]](#cite_note-mls-newdeal2015-300)

Matches not televised nationally were broadcast regionally, often by [regional sports networks](/wiki/Regional_sports_networks "Regional sports networks") like [Bally Sports](/wiki/Bally_Sports "Bally Sports"), [NBC Sports Regional Networks](/wiki/NBC_Sports_Regional_Networks "NBC Sports Regional Networks"), [Spectrum Sports](/wiki/Spectrum_Sports "Spectrum Sports") and [Root Sports](/wiki/Root_Sports "Root Sports"), and sometimes by terrestrial stations like [KTXA](/wiki/KTXA "KTXA"), [WGN](/wiki/WGN_Sports "WGN Sports") and [KMYU](/wiki/KMYU "KMYU").[[58]](#cite_note-latimes1-60) Regionally televised matches were available outside their local markets on [ESPN+](/wiki/ESPN%2B "ESPN+"), which replaced MLS Live from 2018 until 2022.[[296]](#cite_note-301)

### Canada

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Major_League_Soccer&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Canada")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/47/2012_Impact_Montreal_Stade_Saputo.jpg/330px-2012_Impact_Montreal_Stade_Saputo.jpg)](/wiki/File:2012_Impact_Montreal_Stade_Saputo.jpg)

[Montreal Impact](/wiki/CF_Montr%C3%A9al "CF Montréal") hosting [D.C. United](/wiki/D.C._United "D.C. United") (August 2012).

Currently, English-language national MLS broadcast rights in Canada are held by the [TSN](/wiki/The_Sports_Network "The Sports Network") networks through a five-year deal first renewed in 2017. The networks primarily broadcast matches involving the league's Canadian franchises, in combination with separate "regional" rights deals giving TSN exclusive rights to all [Toronto FC](/wiki/Toronto_FC "Toronto FC") and [Vancouver Whitecaps FC](/wiki/Vancouver_Whitecaps_FC "Vancouver Whitecaps FC") matches.[[297]](#cite_note-bell-mlsprwhitecaps-302)[[298]](#cite_note-tsn-whitecapsdeal-303)[[299]](#cite_note-mls-canadiantv17-304) A limited number of matches are also carried by [CTV](/wiki/CTV_Television_Network "CTV Television Network").[[299]](#cite_note-mls-canadiantv17-304)

[TVA Sports](/wiki/TVA_Sports "TVA Sports") held exclusive French-language rights to MLS in Canada from 2017 to 2023. As part of a separate "regional" rights deal, it also held exclusive rights to all [CF Montréal](/wiki/CF_Montr%C3%A9al "CF Montréal") matches .[[299]](#cite_note-mls-canadiantv17-304)[[300]](#cite_note-305)

In 2018, online streaming service [DAZN](/wiki/DAZN "DAZN") obtained the league's digital out-of-market service MLS Live with live and on-demand streaming of matches featuring U.S. teams (matches with Canadian teams were only available after a 48-hour delay to protect the league's main rightsholders TSN and TVA Sports).[[301]](#cite_note-306)

### International

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Major_League_Soccer&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: International")]

MLS also entered into a four-year contract with [Sky Sports](/wiki/Sky_Sports "Sky Sports") to broadcast two MLS matches per week in the United Kingdom and Ireland from 2015 to 2019.[[302]](#cite_note-307) As part of the agreement, Sky Sports broadcast at least two MLS regular season matches each week, as well as the MLS All-Star Game, every MLS Cup Playoff game, and the MLS Cup final. The matches appeared across Sky's family of networks. It also carried weekly MLS highlights across various platforms, including Sky Sports News and SkySports.com. Sky Sports also broadcast at least one match from MLS' "Decision Day" – the final day of the MLS regular season. Many of the matches on Decision Day every year are expected to determine the final spots for the MLS Cup Playoffs.[[303]](#cite_note-308)

[DSport](/wiki/DSport "DSport"), owned by [Discovery Communications](/wiki/Discovery_Communications "Discovery Communications"), began televising league matches in India in 2017.[[304]](#cite_note-309)

[SBS Sport Australia](/wiki/SBS_Sport "SBS Sport") broadcast one MLS game per week in Australia from 2025.[[305]](#cite_note-310)

### Video games

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Major_League_Soccer&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Video games")]

Major League Soccer is a playable league in the [EA Sports FC](/wiki/EA_Sports_FC "EA Sports FC") series, the [eFootball](/wiki/EFootball "EFootball") series, and the [Football Manager](/wiki/Football_Manager "Football Manager") series. The league made its video game debut in 1999 with [FIFA 2000](/wiki/FIFA_2000 "FIFA 2000"). Kids video game company [Humongous Entertainment](/wiki/Humongous_Entertainment "Humongous Entertainment") had the rights to teams and players for their game, [*Backyard Soccer MLS Edition*](/wiki/Backyard_Soccer_MLS_Edition "Backyard Soccer MLS Edition") and for *Backyard Soccer 2004*.[[306]](#cite_note-311)[[307]](#cite_note-312) In 2000, [Konami](/wiki/Konami "Konami") released *[ESPN MLS GameNight](/wiki/ESPN_MLS_GameNight "ESPN MLS GameNight")*, and two years later, they released its sequel, *[ESPN MLS ExtraTime 2002](/wiki/ESPN_MLS_ExtraTime_2002 "ESPN MLS ExtraTime 2002")*. The league made its first appearance in the management series *[Football Manager 2005](/wiki/Football_Manager_2005 "Football Manager 2005")* in 2004.[[308]](#cite_note-313)

## Awards

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Major_League_Soccer&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Awards")]

At the conclusion of each season, the league presents several awards for outstanding achievements, mostly to players, but also to coaches, referees, and teams. The finalists in each category are determined by voting from MLS players, team employees, and the media.[[311]](#cite_note-316)

* [MLS Best XI](/wiki/MLS_Best_XI "MLS Best XI")
* [Sigi Schmid Coach of the Year Award](/wiki/Sigi_Schmid_Coach_of_the_Year_Award "Sigi Schmid Coach of the Year Award")
* [MLS Comeback Player of the Year Award](/wiki/MLS_Comeback_Player_of_the_Year_Award "MLS Comeback Player of the Year Award")
* [MLS Defender of the Year Award](/wiki/MLS_Defender_of_the_Year_Award "MLS Defender of the Year Award")
* [MLS Fair Play Award (individual & team)](/wiki/MLS_Fair_Play_Award "MLS Fair Play Award")
* [MLS Goal of the Year Award](/wiki/MLS_Goal_of_the_Year_Award "MLS Goal of the Year Award")
* [MLS Goalkeeper of the Year Award](/wiki/MLS_Goalkeeper_of_the_Year_Award "MLS Goalkeeper of the Year Award")
* [MLS Golden Boot](/wiki/MLS_Golden_Boot "MLS Golden Boot")
* [MLS Humanitarian of the Year Award](/wiki/MLS_Humanitarian_of_the_Year_Award "MLS Humanitarian of the Year Award")
* [Landon Donovan MVP Award](/wiki/Landon_Donovan_MVP_Award "Landon Donovan MVP Award")
* [MLS Newcomer of the Year Award](/wiki/MLS_Newcomer_of_the_Year_Award "MLS Newcomer of the Year Award")
* [MLS Referee of the Year Award](/wiki/MLS_Referee_of_the_Year_Award "MLS Referee of the Year Award")
* [MLS Young Player of the Year Award](/wiki/MLS_Young_Player_of_the_Year_Award "MLS Young Player of the Year Award")
* [MLS Save of the Year Award](/wiki/MLS_Save_of_the_Year_Award "MLS Save of the Year Award")
